critique.summary: The article discusses the author's experience with AI-assisted coding, describing it as akin to gambling due to its unpredictable and often unreliable outputs. While AI can generate plausible code quickly, the author finds the process unsatisfying and believes it diminishes the deeper intellectual engagement traditionally associated with coding.
mainPoints: AI coding tools can quickly generate code that looks decent but often contains errors or lacks depth.
The author compares using AI for coding to gambling, emphasizing the addictive and uncertain nature of relying on AI-generated outputs.
AI reduces the cognitive challenge and satisfaction derived from coding by offloading thinking and problem-solving to the machine.
Despite AI making the author more confident and encouraging experimentation, it also leads to a sense of laziness and superficial engagement with code.
The author’s personal experience as a developer and designer informs their nuanced view on AI's impact on coding practices.
critique.truthfulness: The article presents a subjective perspective based on the author's personal experience rather than empirical data or multiple sources. The characterization of AI coding as 'gambling' is metaphorical and reflects the author's feelings about unpredictability and reliance on AI outputs. Current AI coding tools like GitHub Copilot and others do generate plausible code snippets but can produce errors or require human oversight, which aligns with the author's claims. However, the article lacks citations or references to broader studies or expert opinions, making it an individual viewpoint rather than a comprehensive factual analysis.
considerations: The article does not address the potential for AI coding tools to improve over time with better training and integration into development workflows.
It overlooks the benefits of AI in reducing repetitive tasks and enabling developers to focus on higher-level design and problem-solving.
The discussion does not consider the diversity of developer experiences; some may find AI tools more empowering than addictive or unsatisfying.
There is no mention of how collaborative team environments might mitigate or amplify the issues raised about AI coding.
The ethical and legal implications of AI-generated code, such as licensing and originality, are not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the metaphor of AI coding as gambling, with many commenters agreeing that while AI introduces variability and uncertainty, it also shifts the developer's role towards verification and specification. Concerns about addiction, quality, and the evolving nature of programming work are also highlighted.

comments.author: mika-el
comments.text:
The gambling analogy is catchy but I think the actual problem is simpler — most people skip the verification step entirely. They see code that compiles and move on.

From my perspective the shift isn't from coding to gambling, it's from writing code to reviewing code. And most engineers were never trained to be good reviewers. We spent years learning how to write, not how to spot what's subtly wrong in something that looks correct.

What helped us was separating the writing and reviewing into different models with different context. One model writes, a different one reviews with explicit knowledge of our codebase conventions. Self-review misses things for the same reason you don't review your own PRs.

The soul part of the article I actually agree with. The satisfaction of figuring something out is real and worth protecting. But I am not sure the answer is to avoid AI — it's to use it for the parts that aren't satisfying and keep the interesting problems for yourself.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully identifies the core challenge with AI-assisted coding as the need for improved review skills, which aligns with known issues in AI code generation and human oversight.

comments.author: cadamsdotcom
comments.text:
> My job went from connecting these two things being the hard and reward part, to just mopping up how poorly they’ve been connected.

That’s only half of the transition.

The other half - and when you know you’ve made it through the “AI sux” phase - is when you learn to automate the mopping up. Give the agent the info it needs to know if it did good work - and if it didn’t do good work, give it information so it knows what to fix. Trust that it wants to fix those things. Automate how that info is provided (using code!) and suddenly you are out of the loop. The amount of code needed is surprisingly small and your agent can write it! Hook a few hundred lines of script up to your harness at key moments, and you will never see dumb AI mistakes again (because it fixed them before presenting the work to you, because your script told it about the mistakes while you were off doing something else)

Think of it like linting but far more advanced - your script can walk the code AST and assess anything, or use regex - your agent will make that call when you ask for the script. If the script has an exit code of 2, stderr is shown to the agent! So you (via your script) can print to stderr what the agent did wrong - what line, what file, wha mistake.

It’s what I do every day and it works (200k LOC codebase, 99.5% AI-coded) - there’s info and ideas here: https://codeleash.dev/docs/code-quality-checks

This is just another technique to engineer quality outcomes; you’re just working from a different starting point.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a practical and accurate approach to integrating AI coding by automating quality checks, reflecting advanced real-world practices.

comments.author: wolttam
comments.text:
I used to write code by hand.

AI has removed some of the tedium, and freed up more of my bandwidth to think about the problems I’m trying to solve and what the actual best ways to solve those problems are.

Only once I have a good feel for the problem I am solving do I go to the AI for help implementing.

My style of prompting usually leads to code that is very close to what I would have manually typed. I review it and tweak it until it is effectively identical to what I would have typed.

The speed up is significant. YMMV.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced perspective on AI as a productivity tool, emphasizing human oversight and the importance of understanding the problem.

comments.author: itsgrimetime
comments.text: All of this new capability has made me realize that the reason i love programming _isn't_ the same as the OP. I used to think (and tell others) that I loved understanding something deeply, wading through the details to figure out a tough problem. but actually, being able to will anything I can think of into existence is what I love about programming. I do feel for the people who were able to make careers out of falling in love w/ and getting good at picking problems & systems apart, breaking them down, and understanding them fully. I respect the discipline, curiosity, and intellect they have. but I also am elated w/ where things are at/going. this feels absurd to say, but I finally feel like I'm _good_ at programming, which is insane, because I literally haven't written a line of code myself in months, but having tools that can finally match the speed my ideas come to me is intoxicating
comments.analysis: A personal reflection that captures the emotional impact of AI on programming identity, though subjective and less focused on technical accuracy.

comments.author: selixe_
comments.text:
I think "gambling" is a bit too strong, but there is a real shift in how we evaluate correctness. With traditional coding, you reason step by step and with AI-assisted code, you're often validating outputs after the fact.

The risk isn't randomness per se it's over trusting something that looks correct. The skill ceiling is moving from "can you write it" to "can you reliably verify it"
comments.analysis: Accurately highlights the evolving skill set required for AI-assisted coding, focusing on verification rather than randomness.

comments.author: dzink
comments.text: It’s variable rewards and even with large models the same question can lead to dramatically different answers. Possibly because they route your request through different models. Possibly because the model has more time to dig through the problem. Nonetheless we have some illusion of control over the output (you we wouldn’t be playing it) but it is just the quality of the model itself that leads to better outcomes - not your input. If you can’t let go of the feeling thought, it’s definitely addictive. And as I look back, it’s a fast iteration on the building cycle we had before AI. But the brain really likes low latency - it is addicted to the fast reward for its actions. So AI, if it gets fast enough (sub 400ms) it will likely become irreversibly addictive to humans in general, as the brain will see is at part of itself. Hope it has our interest at heart by then.
comments.analysis: Provides a plausible psychological perspective on AI coding addiction, linking model behavior to human cognitive biases.

comments.author: Terr_
comments.text:
I'd emphasize that prompting LLMs to generate code isn't just metaphorical gambling in the sense of "taking a risk", the scary part is the more-literal gambling involving addictive behaviors and how those affect the way the user interacts with the machine and the world.

Heck, this technology also offers a parasocial relationship at the same time! Plopping tokens into a slot-machine which also projects a holographic "best friend" that gives you "encouragement" would fit fine in any cyberpunk dystopia.
comments.analysis: Raises valid concerns about the behavioral impact of AI coding tools, though more speculative about addiction mechanisms.

comments.author: watzon
comments.text: I think this article makes a valid point. However, if AI coding is considered gambling, then being a project manager overseeing multiple developers could also be seen as a form of gambling to a certain degree. In reality, there isn't much difference between the two. AI models are non-deterministic, and humans are also non-deterministic. You could assign the same task to two different developers and end up with entirely different results.
comments.analysis: A reasonable analogy that broadens the gambling metaphor to human variability in software development.

comments.author: mika-el
comments.text: depends on bet size. small scoped tasks with tight specs — agents are reliable. "build this feature" with no constraints — yeah that's gambling. I am 90% positive most agent failures I see are from vague task definitions, not model limitations. basically the fix is better scoping not better models
comments.analysis: Accurately identifies task specification as a key factor in AI coding success, a widely accepted viewpoint.

comments.author: Retr0id
comments.text:
> But now either the AI can handle it or it can pretend to handle it. Frankly it's pretending both times, but often it's enough to get the result we need.

This has been how I think about it, too. The success rates are going up, but I still view the AI as an adversary that is trying to trick me into thinking it's being useful. Often the act is good enough to be actually useful, too.
comments.analysis: Reflects a cautious but pragmatic stance on AI coding reliability and the need for human vigilance.

comments.author: amw-zero
comments.analysis: A concise reminder that uncertainty exists in all coding, not just AI-assisted approaches.

comments.author: ljm
comments.text: So AI is halal so long as it makes the solution imperfect.
comments.analysis: A cryptic and likely facetious comment with unclear relevance or constructive value.

comments.author: copypaper
comments.text:
You got to know when to Ship it,

Know when to Re-prompt,

Know when to Clear the Context,

And know when to RLHF.

You never trust the Output,

When you’re staring at the diff view,

There’ll (not) be time enough for Fixing,

When the Tokens are all spent.
comments.analysis: Offers practical advice on managing AI-generated code, emphasizing caution and iterative refinement.

comments.author: hodder
comments.text: Depending on anyone for anything is gambling.
comments.analysis: A philosophical take that broadens the gambling metaphor to all dependencies, somewhat vague.

comments.author: LetsGetTechnicl
comments.text: Yes, that's literally how LLM's work, they're probabilistic.
comments.analysis: A factual statement about the nature of language models, supporting the discussion's foundation.
